title: Simple Adirondack Side Table
summary: A flat-board 1x3 frame table with 1x3 legs screwed to the outside rather than square corner posts — a plainer build than this catalog's pocket-holed Adirondack stool.
description:
This is the plainest possible Adirondack-style side table: a basic frame built from 1x3 boards, with 1x3 legs attached to the outside of the frame rather than square corner posts running through it. **No pocket-hole jig is required at all** — a countersink bit, screws, and glue handle every joint, which is what makes this the faster build of the two small Adirondack-companion tables in this catalog.

Build the basic frame first, in whichever board order is easiest for your countersink setup, then attach the legs to the outside of the frame as shown. **Mark the tabletop boards and dry-fit them on top of the frame before drilling anything** — the top overhangs the frame on purpose, and getting that overhang even on all sides matters more for how the finished table looks than any single joint underneath.

materials.name: Exterior penetrating wood stain or paint

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two frame short sides at 14-1/2", three frame long sides at 15" and four legs at 16-1/4" from the 1x3. Cut five tabletop boards at 18-1/2" from the 1x4. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to two 8 ft 1x3 and one 8 ft 1x4. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw — several parts here are within half an inch of each other and are easy to mix up later.

steps.title: Build the frame
steps.body: Build the basic table frame from the short and long frame sides, using a countersink bit with screws and glue.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Attach the legs to the outside of the frame.

steps.title: Attach the tabletop
steps.body: Mark the tabletop boards and dry-fit on top of the frame for an even overhang on all sides. Predrill and attach.

steps.title: Finish
materials: Exterior penetrating wood stain or paint
